speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is August the 22nd, 1995. My name is Linda Davidson, and I'm interviewing the survivor Faigie Libman, nee Schmidt, in Toronto, Canada. The interview  is being conducted in English. Today's date is August the 22nd, 1995. My name's Linda Davidson, and we are interviewing the survivor Faigie Libman, nee Schmidt, in Toronto, Canada. The interview is being conducted in English. Could you please introduce yourself? Spell your name, maiden name.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My name is Faigie Frances Libman, F-A-I-G-I-E F-R-A-N-C-E-S Libman, L-I-B-M-A-N. I-- my maiden name is Schmidt, S-C-H-M-I-D-T. And I was known as Faigele.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that your birth name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Faigie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Faigele.

speaker: interviewee
text: Faiga.

speaker: interviewer
text: Faiga Schmidt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. But my mother always-- everybody called me Faigele, which means a little bird.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: July 29th, 1934 in Kaunas, some people call it Kovna, Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell Kaunas in Lithuanian?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-A-U-N-A-S, or Kovna, K-O-V-N-A, and Lithuania, L-I-T-H-U-A-N-I-A, Lithuania. And Kovna was the capital in those days. They changed-- Kovna and Vilna used to take turns being the capitals. And they used to call Kovna 'small Paris,' because it was so beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what sense?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very nice city, very bright with boulevards. And the Jewish population was very learned. They believed in learning. I remember always that learning was the most important thing. We had Jewish schools. We had Hebrew high schools. We even had a university, a-- a Jewish hospital, a Jewish orphanage, and one of the most-- in the outskirts, Slabodka was one of the most famous yeshivot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a sense as a young child-- you were born in 1934-- of the Jewish life in Kovna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were aware of your Jewish  identity.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was aware of my Jewish identity, because although I did not-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I spoke at home, my first language was Russian, because-- I was-- I can start telling about that, that my mother was a nurse.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll get to that in a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to stay with this Jewish thing that you started.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The Jewish identity-- I went to a Jewish kindergarten. And Yiddishkeit was, a propos, the most important thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your pre-war address in Kovna?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the name of the street, Ukmergasplantes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope it's still the same thing. U-K-M-E-R-G-A-S, Ukmergas, plantes. That-- I think plantes like over street or a-- a higher grade of street. Plantes, P-L-A-N-T-E-S, plantes, or -A-S. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your home situated in a Jewish area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in a nice part of Kovna.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would say your family was relatively well-to-do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Relatively well-to-do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have in the school that you attended Jewish friends, non-Jewish friends that you played with?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I can remember, Jewish friends, mostly Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'd like to learn about your family. And we could possibly start with your father

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could tell us his full names, date of birth, and his occupation, what kind of man he was. Share with us some of your memories.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my father was one of the nicest men there is. I'm very lucky. I was very lucky, because my mother was always working. She was a nurse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father was-- spoke in those days five languages. The only son with four sisters. He owned an international bookstore. And before he married my mother, he traveled all over the world. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Name. Faiva, F-A-I-V-A. And I was very, very close to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of things you did together?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- you have to understand that the war started in '39 in Poland. By us, it started in '41. So I had like at the age of six and seven a wonderful relationship with my father. My mother was a nurse and she was always working. We went to concerts together. We went to museums together. We went to galleries together. And I was always with my father, very close. I was brought up by a nanny or a governess. And we also had a maid, because my mother was always working. My mother was one of the youngest. And Jewish girls, maybe out of 500, 2 or 3 were taken to be nurses. She was my first career woman that I knew of.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you a little bit more about your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the kind of family that he came from.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He came-- in fact, my paternal grandparents lived down the street. Ukmergasplantes was a little bit like a-- a little hilly. And I remember going to visit them. They lived down the street from us. My father was-- had four sisters. One, Tante [? Liana ?] went away to-- before the war to Paris. She married a count. And then Tante [? Nadia ?] went to Israel. Tante [? Liana ?] had a chemistry degree. Tante [? Nadia ?] was a pharmacist. She went to Israel. She emigrated to Israel. And Tante [? Chaya, ?] just before the war-- she was a bookkeeper-- went to Buenos Aires. So there was only one sister left, [? Chava. ?] She was still at university.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they all were university educated.

speaker: interviewee
text: All very, very educated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father too, very educated. In fact, my-- he wanted to-- not to go back to Lithuania, to Kovna. But the story goes that my-- my grandmother said that she lost her teeth worrying about my father. He was the only son.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he returned to Lithuania. He traveled to Paris. He traveled-- I remember all the stories telling me all the adventures he had in Spain. And he was a very interesting man.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he introduced me in the-- to have the love of books. We used to spend hours together. My biggest thrill was in my father's store looking through books. And that was from, as far as I can remember, '39 to '41 being with him all the time, being like his best friend, because like when the Russians came in 1940, it wasn't in style anymore to have like a governess or a  maid. You see, they also took over the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in your store, you mentioned that it was an international bookstore.

speaker: interviewee
text: International bookstore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the language that you spoke with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And people coming into the shop--

speaker: interviewee
text: They spoke Lithuanian and Russian too, because, you see, I still remember the Russian language, because-- actually speaking, because from the governess, that was my first language introduced into Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: From a Jewish point of view, was your father observant in what sense?

speaker: interviewee
text: Traditional, not Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: It means that on the holidays, they went to shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember going with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember going with him. My mother was always working. Her-- nursing was her life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your-- what is your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name is Batia Chaimovitz, maiden name Chaimovitz, and my-- and Schmidt. And now it's Malamud-- M-A-L-A-M-U-D. And as far as I can remember, she was the Jewish General in-- a surgical nurse. And in fact, the things that I remember getting-- looking at telegrams when I was much older, that everybody was so excited. I was born during the summer. And a pile of telegrams came, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That means that we're wishing you from [? Birston-- ?] was a very famous resort place that doctors and nurses went. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] means that I should get married and I didn't-- wouldn't need a dowry. And I also remember we got the most gorgeous-- not a chandelier, but something like a chandelier in the dining room, because everybody-- she was a very known nurse. She was very well-liked. She was always the general. She was always in charge.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that she was employed at the Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a Jewish hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Jewish hospital in Kovna before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So  how early after your birth did your mother return to work? Would you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. But as far as I can remember, very soon. Like nursing was her life, because, you have to understand, she was born in Krakinova. That's a small town. She studied in Panevezys. Her parents sent her, which was a very big thing. It's not like nowadays. You just go to another school. They sent her. She boarded with a lady. And they sent her to Panevezys. And from Panevezys, they sent her to Kovna to study.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that this was very unusual for women in that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. For women, it was unusual for a woman to become-- to-- to become a career woman. And she had to study very hard, because just a few were taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that accounted for the fact that you were an only child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could be. Yes. Could be. Could be. The only time I was with-- like, I used to see my-- more of my mother when we went to Memel. Memel had also a famous resort town. And we used to go to visit my mother's parents. They lived in Krakinova. So then I was like more-- she had vacation time. But otherwise, I was with a governess, or in kindergarten, or with my father during the Russian occupation, during the Russian--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the relationship that you had with your mother, would you describe that as not close?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not close. The war made us close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your Russian governess.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a student, a very nice girl. And this is how come I spoke Russian and Lithuanian too. And then we had a maid too, because my mother was always working.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a governess for how many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ooh, probably from '34-- maybe from '35 'til '39, 'til '40, because the Russians came in in '40.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a common thing for people to have governesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so. I also had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a special room in our house that a seamstress used to come and make my clothes. I was, I suppose, nowadays you would call spoiled. I don't remember being spoiled. I had clothes made especially for me. I remember having a seal coat with a hat and a muff to match, which all those clothes came in very handy when we were in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll talk about that a bit later.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'll talk about that. I had beautiful clothes, embroidered little blouses with this-- with-- like a-- all with hand embroidery. I was very-- I was the apple of everybody's eye, because my-- my paternal grandparents didn't have any grandchildren. Only later on, [? Yehuda ?] that was born in Israel-- he's younger than I am-- he was the only one. And then Tante [? Chaya ?] had a little girl in Buenos Aires. But I was the only one. And my parents, my mother was the youngest one. So I was also there the whole-- the whole to do. They used to make a very big fuss over me. My birthday was in July. I remember they-- we went into the woods and they decorated the whole woods with balloons and with streamers, because my friends were not there. I had to have a birthday in July with my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that feel like walking into the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- it was-- the only thing, I missed my friends from school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was during vacation time.

speaker: interviewee
text: During vacation time, I was with my maternal grandparents. They were also-- they had orchards. They were also well-to-do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are the kind of things that you did with your governess?

speaker: interviewee
text: She used to take me to school. I also started piano lessons. And when I got a little bit older, I remember during the Russian occupation in  1940 walking down to the teacher. I had everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what age did you start school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I suppose in '39 at the age of five and then continued 'til '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of a school did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I cannot.

speaker: interviewer
text: The address?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I only remember a choir that we were-- like when the Russians came in, they tried to-- the culture was very high in singing and in learning, that I remember joining a choir, being a pitzel, a little one, joining a choir and singing all the songs that were-- and I remember even once being so excited in talking to people, to my friends, that the choir master asked me to leave. I remember that. That was-- and then I joined it again. He didn't-- for one day, he didn't let me. And I-- I had a very-- also my mother, a very strict upbringing. It's all before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the language of instruction at this Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, but Lithuanian too. Later on, it was Lithuanian. And when the Russians came, Russian too. But I was a big shot. I spoke already Russian then, so I learned all the Russian songs. I-- 'til this day I still remember 'kalinka maya' and the anthem of the-- the Russian anthem, because I learned it at a very young age, because I already spoke some Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your favorite subjects at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I can remember, math. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Math. And I must have been-- so if in -- I was only in grade 1 and 2, you see? So it's-- I don't remember much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as a child, what were your hobbies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reading and playing with friends. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the choir. Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the choir. And also, I received-- I don't remember from which relative, I think on my mother's side, a Shirley Temple doll that I had a very close friend-- her name was Chaya-- and we used to share it. And for hours, we used to share. I was the envy of everybody. I had a Shirley Temple doll. I think it must have been my mother's sister sending me, because my mother's sisters-- one moved to New Rochelle and the other one to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned this wonderful childhood with all the material--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and Closeness with your father. Did you have any sense of aloneness towards your mother? Was this an issue for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I was-- I was very satisfied. I think this nature lasted-- even in my old age now, I was very positive. I remember-- and I will tell you afterwards stories for during the war-- I was very positive. Always, I used to-- always with a smile-- and I loved life. I enjoyed every moment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's-- I don't remember anything. I remember being a little bit scared of her. That is the truth, because she was such an important person-- Efficient.

speaker: interviewee
text: Efficient and so that as far as I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who cooked in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not my-- probably the maid. Maybe my mother did on her days off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the smells from the cooking in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. I remember other things like going to galleries and going singing, and-- I don't remember. We-- I probably ate quite a bit at my paternal grandparents. They lived right down the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember your house that you could describe it to us as a child's impression?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very clean. Terribly clean. My mother was a-- insisted on cleanliness. I remember very clean, very pretty. I had a very nice room. My parents had a very nice room. We had a salon. And we also had, as I said, a sewing room. That I remember because I spent time with the lady that used to come to sew my clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the governess live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. She didn't. I don't think so. But she was there all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned a lot your paternal-- your father's family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I was very close. I don't remember, as I said before, the sisters who went away. I only remember Tante [? Chaya, ?] because she left just before the outbreak of the war to Buenos Aires and Tante [? Chaya ?] and Tante [? Evelyn, ?] [? Chava, ?] Tante [? Chaya ?] and Tante Chava, I-- because she was very young, so it's like she was my best friend too. And my paternal grandparents, I used to visit them. My grandfather was a very sweet man. My grandmother was very a matriarch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were--his name was [? Richesko. ?] And hers was [? Nava-- ?] after [? Nava, ?] but it was Yetta.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall celebrating Shabbats and the high holidays with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, with them. With them. And-- mostly with them. I remember going to visit my grandparents in Krakinova, but mostly with them. I was much closer with them than with my maternal grandparents, although I spent longer times with my maternal ones, like at a stretch.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of recall do you have of a Shabbat, a typical Shabbat, or  a Jewish holiday with your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. With my grandparents-- with my paternal-- very, very not-- quite lively, but dignified. They were very-- the Schmidts were very dignified people.

speaker: interviewer
text: I had more fun I think in-- in Krakinova, where everybody let their hair down. You know what I mean? We were singing and dancing. The other side was more [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dignified, more austere. And would you have attended the synagogue with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other childhood memories of your parents that you would like to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very loving. I mean, the good times-- we had good times. I was a little kid. I don't-- I was very happy. I remember being happy. I remember going like walking on the sidewalks not walking, like skipping, skipping a lot. I was very happy. The only time I remember that must have been '39 and '40, when certain refugees came into-- from Poland into Lithuania. Maybe they snuck in or whatever. And I remember people being in our house with my father. He was very well-liked. He was a very kind and known person in Kovna. So-- and I remember my parents, when I used to walk in, stopping to talk. You know, you don't tell the child anything. So this is why when in '41, when the war broke out, it was a real shock, because we were happy. The Russians, I enjoyed them. They brought in a lot of extra culture. I don't have any bad memories from '40 to '41. I was seven years old. I had friends galore. I had singing and dancing that I enjoy to this day. I was the happiest. I don't-- piano, all things, playing with dolls, playing with-- a big shot. I had-- I don't remember anybody else having a Shirley Temple doll.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did mention that when the Russians occupied Lithuania, the one effect was that you lost your governess.

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost my governess.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other one was that your father's shop--

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, so my-- he still went to work, but it wasn't his own. They-- you know, they-- they took it over.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect him? Did you sense any difference in him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember. I don't remember any difference. I remember the difference that he had more time with me. You see? Somebody else was there also. We were more together. We were always together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Russian occupation affect you, do you think, financially in any sense?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could be. Could be. But I don't remember. We had everything. They weren't there so long that it should affect us. Whatever we had, we still enjoyed all the things that were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Under Russian occupation-- I know you were a young girl-- but was there any sense of anti-Semitism or anti-Jewish feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember. I don't remember. All that came out when the Germans came in. And that was horrendous.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this play out? How did a child of seven view the occupation of Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: The occupation of Lithuania by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Germans. I remember it was spring. My mother being, again-- oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941. I remember her at work. And we had a telephone. In those days, we had a Phillips radio. We had a telephone. She was at-- in the hospital. I remember noise and bombs falling all over. And my mother called us. And we joined her in the hospital. For three days, the bombs were flying. And the wounded were-- it was unbelievable. My father helped out. He had a very soft heart. And even I did. The hospital was filled with wounded. I remember that very clearly. And for three days and nights-- again, I suppose I was safer in the hospital with my mother and my father. And then when the bombs stopped, the Germans entered. And that's when our problems started. My--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a bunker in the hospital where you slept?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your accommodation there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We slept in the wards with the-- with the wounded. We were supposed to be there to help out, to help out because it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a child of seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a child of seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of things did you do to help out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I probably bring bandages, probably make myself useful. I don't remember even seeing a lot of my mother. She was like-- she was busy. She was-- don't forget, she was a surgical nurse. And she was a very good one. We were there for safety, so we did everything possible to stay, because we felt that the hospital was the safest place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any contact with your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That-- those three days, we were just alone. And then, when we came back, right away naturally, they were all right. We were-- my mother came home with us. We stayed home. And-- it started. The Lithuanians were very helpful. Every Jewish-- every Jew-- my name is Schmidt. If they wouldn't have mixed in, I could have saved-- we could have saved some time without being under the German occupation. They-- right away the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you elaborate on that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were helpers. The Lithuanians were very big helpers. We lost 90% of the Jews. The most people that lost-- that lost during the war were the Lithuanian Jews, because the Lithuanians were very big helpers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by that? Helping who?

speaker: interviewee
text: To help the Germans. To help the Germans. They rounded up the people. They helped. There was a Lithuanian-- like a-- Lithuanian soldiers, Lithuanian Army. They-- the intellectuals were rounded up. I remember again phoning my mother that my father was taken away.
